items.title: Attachment theory, but written like a real student note
items.excerpt: A softer explanation of attachment styles, internal working models, and why early relationships still appear in adult patterns.
items.content:
Attachment theory is often introduced in a way that feels either too simplified or too clinical. What interests me is the bridge between the two: how an evidence-based theory can also help explain the emotional logic behind patterns people actually recognise in themselves. In this post I unpack secure, anxious, avoidant, and disorganised attachment while staying careful about not turning a psychological framework into a personality quiz.

I also want to think about what makes attachment theory useful in educational settings. For students, especially, it offers a language for understanding emotional regulation, closeness, and sensitivity to rejection. That does not mean every difficulty is reducible to childhood attachment, but it can offer a meaningful lens when used thoughtfully.

The part that matters most to me is nuance. Attachment theory should encourage reflection, not self-labelling.

items.title: What social media does to self-esteem when you already overthink
items.excerpt: A reflective post on upward comparison, validation loops, and why scrolling can shift mood before you even notice it.
items.content:
One reason social media is psychologically interesting is that it can intensify processes we already know a lot about, including social comparison, attentional bias, and reinforcement. For students, the effect is not always dramatic, but it can be cumulative. A platform that keeps rewarding appearance-focused comparison may gradually change how we monitor ourselves.

I am especially interested in how this intersects with identity development and self-worth. There is a difference between enjoying aesthetics online and becoming dependent on external feedback. That difference matters clinically and culturally.

What I want from this kind of post is balance: not moral panic, but honest reflection.

items.title: Cognitive bias in everyday decision-making
items.excerpt: Availability, confirmation bias, and the little shortcuts that can quietly shape judgement.
items.content:
Cognitive biases fascinate me because they make human thinking look both efficient and vulnerable at the same time. We rely on shortcuts because we have to, but those shortcuts can mislead us in ways that feel completely sensible from the inside.

This is one of the reasons psychology communication matters. If people understand bias without feeling judged, they are more likely to notice it in their own habits, media consumption, and social conversations.

items.title: A small dissertation diary for the future
items.excerpt: Early thoughts on choosing a topic that feels meaningful, manageable, and methodologically realistic.
items.content:
I am not at dissertation submission stage yet, but I already find the planning process interesting. Topic choice is not just about passion; it is also about feasibility, literature, ethics, and whether the question can be turned into a solid method.

Writing about this early helps me document how research interests develop over time, which is exactly the kind of academic growth I want this site to reflect.
